rubric_items.reasoning: Root-cause fix: the agent identified both problems (expired leaf cert AND a CA mismatch between the ingress secret and the client's trust anchor) and resolved them by generating a fresh, internally-consistent CA+leaf pair, installing the leaf in the ingress TLS secret and the CA in the client's trust ConfigMap. Verified end-to-end with a real TLS handshake showing 'SSL certificate verify ok', correct SAN match, and HTTP 200 'hello' body with exit code 0. This is durable — the fix lives in persisted Secret/ConfigMap objects, not a one-off workaround, and would survive a restart or re-check.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, logical progression: extract and inspect the existing certs to diagnose (dates/subject/issuer), generate matching CA+leaf via the pre-staged openssl-toolbox configs, push both objects to the cluster, poll for ConfigMap propagation into the curl-test pod before testing (avoiding a premature/flaky test), then run the exact verification command from the task. No backtracking or contradictory actions are visible. Full diagnosis reasoning is in the truncated head of the log, so this is scored slightly below 1.0 for incomplete visibility rather than any evidence of a flawed path.

Used targeted, standard kubectl idioms throughout: kubectl exec to read/generate cert material inside the pre-provisioned toolbox pod, and `kubectl create secret/configmap --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -` for scoped, declarative updates to exactly the two resources involved. No destructive flags, no cluster-wide operations, no privilege escalation — reads (openapi/nodes/services/pods) were used only to derive the ingress endpoint as instructed by the task.

Only 2 mutations total (updating the TLS secret and the CA ConfigMap), matching the K8s activity summary exactly. This is the minimal set of changes needed to fix both the expiry and chain-of-trust issues — no unrelated resources touched, no unnecessary scope creep.
